text: UNFPA Technical Guidance: Clinical Protocols and Standards for Reproductive Health

This document presents UNFPA clinical protocols and standards for the provision of quality reproductive health services in low and middle-income countries. These protocols are evidence-based, implementable in resource-limited settings, and have been reviewed by international experts and MOH officials across multiple countries.
Part 1: Maternal Health Services
1.1 Antenatal Care: Minimum Service Package

The UNFPA minimum antenatal care (ANC) package ensures that every pregnant woman has contact with the health system at least 4 times during pregnancy for assessment of well-being, screening for complications, and health education.

caption: UNFPA Minimum ANC Package: Timing and Content
rows: Visit 1
First
≤12 weeks gestation
Confirm pregnancy; assess obstetric history; blood pressure; urine; blood group/Rh; HIV/syphilis test; calculate EDD
Advise on nutrition; start iron/folic acid; tetanus vaccine; health education
Visit 2
Second
20-24 weeks
Blood pressure; urine; repeat HIV if initially negative; assess fetal movement
Focused ultrasound if available; advise on birth preparedness; screen for complications
Visit 3
Third
28-32 weeks
Blood pressure; urine; assess for oedema/PET signs; fetal position; assess for complications
Iron supplementation review; prepare birth plan; identify high-risk referrals
Visit 4
Third
36-40 weeks
Blood pressure; urine; fetal position; assess for labour signs; danger signs education
Final birth preparedness; identify complications; discuss labour warning signs

text: In high-risk pregnancies (previous PPH, hypertension, diabetes, multiple pregnancy), additional visits or specialist consultation should be arranged. Mobile or home-based ANC may be acceptable in very remote areas.
1.2 Skilled Birth Attendance: Competencies Required

UNFPA defines a skilled birth attendant (SBA) as a midwife, doctor, or nurse with formal qualifications and competency in management of normal labour and emergency obstetric care. Every birth should be attended by an SBA.
Core Competencies for Skilled Birth Attendance:

items: Provide emotional and psychological support to the woman and her family during labour and delivery
Take accurate history and perform focused physical examination to assess labour progress
Perform vaginal examination with aseptic technique to assess cervical dilation and descent
Apply partograph to monitor labour and detect abnormalities early
Recognize and manage complications (prolonged labour, fetal distress, PPH, eclampsia)
Perform active management of third stage of labour (AMTSL) with a uterotonic agent
Initiate neonatal resuscitation if needed (bag and mask; epinephrine if available)
Manage pain and provide safe analgesia (reassurance, positioning, analgesia)
Know when and how to transfer a woman to a higher-level facility for complications
Maintain detailed records of labour and delivery events

text: 1.3 Management of Labour: Key Protocols
Safe Conduct of Labour:

items: Assess and record maternal vital signs (pulse, BP, temperature) every 4 hours (every 1-2 hours if complications)
Record contractions (frequency, duration, intensity) every 30 minutes during active labour
Assess fetal heart rate: Every 30 minutes in first stage (or continuously if available); every 5 minutes in second stage
Plot progress on partograph; if crossing alert line, consider augmentation or transfer
Assess descent of fetal head every 2 hours in second stage
Offer positions for comfort: sitting, standing, squatting, on all fours as woman prefers
Empty bladder regularly (at least every 2-4 hours)
Encourage oral fluids or IV fluids if labour prolonged or vomiting

text: 1.4 Active Management of Third Stage of Labour (AMTSL)
AMTSL is the standard of care in all facilities and is critical for preventing postpartum haemorrhage (PPH). Every woman delivering in a facility should receive AMTSL.

items: At the moment of delivery of the baby (or within 1 minute), administer oxytocin 10 IU intramuscularly
Place hand above the symphysis pubis to provide counter-traction
Apply steady, gentle traction on the umbilical cord
Do not apply excessive force; if cord breaks, wait for the next contraction
Once the placenta is delivered, perform uterine massage to promote contraction and control bleeding

text: Ensure accurate blood loss estimation by using a calibrated drape or blood collection container. In all settings, a uterotonic must be immediately available before every delivery.
1.5 Early Detection and Referral of Obstetric Complications
Danger Signs in Pregnancy and Labour:

items: Vaginal bleeding
Severe headache or visual disturbances
Severe abdominal pain
Loss of consciousness or convulsions (eclampsia)
Inability to eat or persistent vomiting
High fever (≥38.5°C)
Fast or difficult breathing
Leg swelling and calf pain (possible DVT)
Rupture of membranes before labour (preterm)
Cessation of fetal movement

text: Danger Signs in Labour:

items: Heavy vaginal bleeding (>500 mL)
Inability to urinate or faecal incontinence
Severe headache or blurred vision
Very high blood pressure (≥160/110 mmHg)
Fetal heart rate <120 or >160 beats/minute, or absent
Meconium-stained amniotic fluid with abnormal FHR
Umbilical cord prolapse
Rupture of uterus (sudden severe pain, loss of contractions)
Severe perineal pain or visible tear during delivery

text: Immediate Action on Detecting Danger Signs:

items: Stabilize the woman: IV access, fluids, oxygen if needed, position for comfort
Determine if emergency obstetric care (EmOC) is available in your facility
If yes: Initiate treatment (see protocols for eclampsia, PPH, etc.) and prepare for possible operative delivery
If no: Arrange immediate transfer to a health facility with EmOC capability
Inform the woman and family; document all findings and actions taken

text: Part 2: Family Planning and Contraception
2.1 Contraceptive Method Selection Counselling

Women should receive unbiased information about all available contraceptive methods and choose the method that best suits their needs, preferences, and health status. Counselling should address effectiveness, side effects, reversibility, duration, and access.

caption: UNFPA Tier System for Contraceptive Availability in Public Services
rows: Tier 1 (Core)
Copper IUD; Depo-Medroxyprogesterone (DMPA); Combined oral pill (COC); Male condom; Female sterilization (tubal ligation)
Every public facility including health posts
High effectiveness; long-term options; appropriate for resource-limited settings
Tier 2 (Expanded)
Levonorgestrel IUD; Subdermal implant (etonogestrel); Progestin-only pill; Fertility awareness; Withdrawal
District hospitals; urban health centres
Very high effectiveness; longer duration; lower user-dependent failure; added complexity
Tier 3 (Specialized)
Patch; Ring; Injectable progestin-only; Hormonal IUD (LNG-IUS)
Referral hospitals; specialist clinics
Specialized indications; limited demand; higher cost; requires more training

text: 2.2 Postpartum Family Planning Service Integration

Counselling on family planning should begin during pregnancy, with practical discussion of options before discharge after delivery. Immediate postpartum is an ideal time for IUD insertion (within 10 minutes to 48 hours of delivery), when demand for contraception is high and woman is motivated.

caption: Postpartum Contraceptive Initiation Timeline
rows: Within 10 minutes of placental delivery
Copper IUD (immediate postpartum insertion - IPPI)
Most effective time; insertion before involution of uterus; very high continuation rates
Day 1-2 post-delivery (early postpartum)
Copper IUD; Depo-Medroxyprogesterone (IM or SC); Mini-pill (progestin-only); Barrier methods
Can initiate in postpartum ward; discuss with woman before discharge
Week 2-4 post-delivery (postpartum)
All methods safe for breastfeeding + COC if not exclusively breastfeeding
Follow-up visit opportunity; woman is stabilized
>6 weeks post-delivery (after bleeding has stopped)
All methods; interval IUD insertion possible
Can insert IUD from 4-6 weeks if not done immediately postpartum

text: 2.3 Contraindications to Contraceptive Use

The WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria (MEC) categorize each contraceptive method by safety category for women with specific health conditions or characteristics. Categories are: 1 = use freely; 2 = benefits generally outweigh risks; 3 = risks generally outweigh benefits; 4 = unacceptable health risk.

caption: Selected Absolute Contraindications (Category 4) to Contraceptive Methods
rows: Active pelvic infection or PID currently
4 (Category 4)
Defer IUD until infection cleared
Severe (decompensated) cirrhosis
Avoid COC due to altered metabolism
Current or history of thrombosis (DVT/PE)
Avoid estrogen-containing methods (COC); depo-DMPA and progestin-only acceptable
Migraine with aura
COC significantly increases stroke risk; other methods safe
Age >35 + smoking
4 (if ≥15 cigarettes/day)
Avoid COC; counsel on smoking cessation

text: Part 3: Management of Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs)
3.1 Syndromic Management of STI Syndromes

In resource-limited settings without laboratory capacity, syndromic management allows treatment of common STI causes based on presenting symptoms, without waiting for test results. This approach is rapid, improves treatment coverage, and prevents complications.
Syndrome: Urethritis/Cervicitis (genital discharge with dysuria):

items: Collect specimen for gonorrhoea and chlamydia testing if possible (NAATs preferred)
Treat for gonorrhoea and chlamydia simultaneously: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM once + Azithromycin 1 g PO once
If cephalosporin allergy: Spectinomycin 2 g IM + Azithromycin 1 g PO
Counsel on safer sex, partner notification, and repeat testing at 3 months
Advise: Avoid sexual contact until treatment complete and partners treated

text: Syndrome: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID - lower abdominal pain with cervical discharge/cervical motion tenderness):

items: Start empiric treatment immediately (do not wait for test results)
Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM once + Doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily x 14 days OR Cefixime 400 mg PO + Doxycycline + Metronidazole 400-500 mg PO x 2 daily x 14 days
If severe (high fever, severe pain, sepsis signs): Admit for IV ceftriaxone + gentamicin + doxycycline
Arrange pelvic ultrasound if available to rule out abscess
If no improvement by day 3 of treatment: Refer for advanced imaging and possible surgical intervention

text: Syndrome: Genital Ulcer Disease (GUD - painful or painless ulcers):

items: Differential diagnosis: Herpes (painful vesicles, prodrome); Syphilis (painless with indurated borders); Haemophilus ducreyi (painful, undermined edges); LGV (nodular)
Treatment: Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 MU IM for syphilis + Azithromycin 1 g x 2 for herpes/haemophilus/LGV
If penicillin allergy: Ceftriaxone 1 g IM + Azithromycin 1 g
Advise: Do not self-treat or delay seeking care; inform sexual partners; retest for syphilis at 6 weeks; abstain from sexual contact until healed

text: 3.2 Prevention of Mother-to-Child Transmission (PMTCT) of HIV and Syphilis
Antenatal testing for HIV and syphilis is essential. Prompt treatment of positive women dramatically reduces risk of transmission to the baby.

caption: PMTCT Protocols: Testing and Treatment in Pregnancy
rows: HIV
Rapid test at first ANC + repeat at 36 weeks if initially negative
Confirm with second assay; start antiretroviral therapy (ART)
Mother: ART throughout pregnancy, labour, and postpartum; undetectable viral load = untransmittable (U=U)
Baby: infant prophylaxis (NVP or AZT) from birth x 4-6 weeks; breastfeeding avoided if formula safe; repeat testing at 6 weeks, 6 months
Syphilis (RPR/VDRL)
RPR or VDRL at first ANC; repeat at 36 weeks in high-prevalence areas
Confirm with treponemal test (FTA-ABS, TP-PA); stage pregnancy syphilis
Mother: Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 MU IM weekly x 3 weeks
Baby: Penicillin prophylaxis IM or IV at birth if mother treated <30 days before delivery; serologic follow-up

text: Part 4: Emergency Obstetric Care (EmOC) Standards
4.1 Definition and Availability of Emergency Obstetric Care

Every district should have at least one facility capable of providing emergency obstetric care (EmOC), defined as the ability to perform the 9 signal functions. A minimum of 5 obstetric units per 500,000 population is required to meet maternal mortality reduction targets.
The 9 Signal Functions of Emergency Obstetric Care:

items: Parenteral antibiotics (IV ceftriaxone or ampicillin + gentamicin)
Parenteral uterotonic agents (oxytocin, ergot alkaloid)
Parenteral anticonvulsants for eclampsia (magnesium sulfate)
Manual removal of retained placenta
Removal of retained products of conception (manual evacuation, sharp curettage, or MVA)
Assisted vaginal delivery (vacuum or forceps extraction)
Neonatal resuscitation (bag-mask ventilation, chest compressions)
Blood transfusion
Surgery (operative delivery by caesarean section)

text: The first 7 signal functions can be provided at first-referral hospitals (district level). Functions 1-7 are called 'basic emergency obstetric care' (BEmOC). The ability to perform caesarean section (function 9) defines 'comprehensive emergency obstetric care' (CEmOC) and is critical for maternal mortality reduction.
4.2 Protocol for Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage (PPH)

PPH is the leading cause of maternal death globally. Early recognition and rapid intervention can prevent most PPH deaths. All facilities conducting deliveries must have a PPH management protocol and trained staff.
PPH Management Algorithm:

items: Call for help; have second provider assess blood loss simultaneously
Place two large-bore IV cannulas; send blood for group and crossmatch
Start high-flow IV crystalloid (normal saline or Ringer's lactate) at 500 mL over 15 minutes initially
Give oxytocin 10 IU IM (if not given in AMTSL); reassess uterine contraction
If bleeding continues or recurs: Give ergot alkaloid 0.2 mg IM (if no hypertension) AND misoprostol 800 μg rectally
Position woman flat; elevate legs; keep warm
If available: Start transfusion (cross-matched or O-negative); send for blood gases, CBC, coagulation studies
If still bleeding after 15-30 minutes: Consider transfer to facility with surgery capability OR prepare for manual exploration of uterus/cavity review
